with f and g,
I want something about h,

and I get that f(x_1) + g(x_1)
is less than f(x_2) + g(x_2).
But now look what we have!
f(x_1) + g(x_1) is h(x_1),

Notice how I treated VERY differently
f and g on one hand, and h on the other.
For h, I PROVED that it was increasing;
but for f and g, I ASSUMED
and I used throughout my proof
that they were increasing.
That's the difference
between a hypothesis and the conclusion.

Proof by induction
is a type of proof
that works particularly well
for certain theorems.
I will explain it with an example.
I want to prove
that for every natural number n
greater than or equal to 4,
n! is greater than 2^n.
If I only wanted to prove this
for one value of n,
for example for n = 4, 
it would be very easy.
Just do the calculation and check it.
But I have infinitely many values,
so I CANNOT do it one by one.
If I had a way to prove it
for ALL values of n at once,
I would do that; 
it would probably be the fastest.
But I can't think
of how to do that in this case.
So instead, I'm going to try
to write a proof by induction.
I'm going to call the inequality,
the thing I want to prove, "S_n."
This is just so I can refer to it easily.
And in general,
proof by induction
is well-suited for certain theorems
that depend on a natural number,
like this one.
Here is the structure
of a proof by induction.
It has two steps.
The first one is called the "base case."
I need to prove S_n
for the smallest value of n
-- in this case, 4.
So I just need to prove S_4.
The second step is the "induction step."
I need to prove that for every n
greater than or equal to 4,
S_n implies S_(n+1).
So that means I'm going to be proving
that S_4 implies S_5,
S_5 implies S_6,
S_6 implies S_7,
and so on.
And I don't need to do anything else.
So, I need to prove the statement
for one value of n, n = 4,
and then I need to prove
a bunch of implications.
To be clear,
in the induction step
I am NOT proving S_n
and I am NOT proving S_(n+1).
I am ONLY proving
that one statement, S_n,
implies another statement, S_(n+1).
So in this case,
I will be proving
that one inequality
implies another inequality.
By itself,
the induction step doesn't tell me
whether ANY of the statements are true.
If I ONLY prove the induction step,
it would still be possible
for ALL the S_n's to be FALSE.
However, when I prove BOTH the base case
AND the induction step,
what can I conclude?
Well, because I've proven the base case, 
I know S_4 is true.
And then, 
because I've proved an induction step,
I know that S_4 implies S_5,
so S_5 is also true.
I know S_5 implies S_6,
so I know S_6 is also true.
I know S_6 implies S_7,
so I know S_7 is also true.
And so on.
And I will have proven the statement
for all values of n, as I wanted.
To summarize once more,
I proved the statement for ONE value of n 
-- that's it --
and then I proved
a BUNCH of implications,
and the two things together
are enough to conclude
that the statement is true
for all values of n.
Enough introduction.
Let's write down the proof.

To conclude,

It consists of proving
that the statement for n
IMPLIES the statement for n + 1;
it's an implication.
going back to how I set it up here,

When we say
that a function "is continuous”,
we mean that
we can "sketch its graph in one go”;
without lifting the pen from the paper.

not rigorous, but it helps.
For example, this graph CAN be sketched
without lifting the pen from the paper.
I want to transform this vague idea
into a rigorous definition.

that CANNOT be drawn “in one go”,
and see why.
This graph, for example,
CANNOT be sketched
without lifting pen from paper,
because it has a hole.
Notice that it does not matter
whether the function
is UNdefined at the point,
or it is defined “with the wrong value,"
so to speak.
Either way, I want to say
that this function is not continuous.
This example is different.
It has a jump, so I cannot sketch it
without lifting pen from paper, either.
Neither can I this one,
because it has a vertical asymptote.
And finally, I cannot sketch this one
without lifting pen from paper, either.
I can sketch the graph fine
on EITHER side of x = 0,
but I CANNOT get through 0,
because the function oscillates wildly.
I want to say that all these examples
are not continuous.
Many of these examples of bad functions
have one thing in common:
they lack a limit.
This function does not have a limit
as x approaches 0.
THIS function does not have a limit
as x approaches 0.
and THIS function does not have a limit
as x approaches 1.
However, THIS example is different.
The function in this graph
HAS a limit as x approaches a.
But it has a hole,

On the other hand, THIS function
has a limit as x approaches a,

are different,

Let's put all these examples together.
This suggests how I want
to define continuity.
I will say 
that a function f is continuous at a
when the limit of f(x)
as x approaches a
is equal to the value
of the function f at a.
All the examples I constructed,
where I could not sketch the graph
of the function
without lifting pen from paper,
failed THIS,
so this seems like a good definition.
To make this into a precise definition,
I have to introduce my variables,
I will say that a is a real number,
and I will require f to be a function
defined at least
on an interval centered at a,
so that I can study its limit at a.
To be very explicit,
this definition means three things.
1. The limit must exist
and therefore be a number.
2. The function must be defined.
And 3. The limit must be equal
to the value of the function.
Notice that both the limit
and the function must be the same NUMBER.
So for example,
if they both do not exist,
then they do not satisfy the definition.
And there we have it!
This is the definition of continuity.
This was the simplest way
to write the definition,
but there is an alternative,
equivalent way:
we can use the (ε, δ)-definition of limit
EXPLICITLY.

this is the definition
of “the limit as x approaches a of f(x)
is L”.
For a function to be continuous,
the limit must not be just any number L,
but specifically f(a).
So let’s simply change L into f(a).
And now we can do a simplification.
In the definition of LIMIT
we must exclude the case x = a,
because we don't even CARE
whether the function is defined at a.
but when we want CONTINUITY,
the function MUST be defined at a,
and the limit must be f(a),

This condition at the bottom,
when we remove the exclusion x = a,
is entirely equivalent.

In this video, I will present
a VERY important and useful theorem
about continuity.
It is so useful
that you have probably already used it,
even if you had not realized it.

a function f is continuous at a point
when calculating the limit
is the same as evaluating.
Not all functions
are continuous everywhere.
If they were, computing limits
would be much easier!
But a LOT of common functions
ARE continuous,
and THAT is what this theorem says.
Specifically, 
any function we can construct
with sum, product,
quotient, and composition
of polynomials, roots,
trigonometric functions, exponentials,
logarithms, and absolute values
is continuous -- on its domain.
This is a lot of functions,
and they are the functions
that we use most often.
Sometimes, these functions
are called “elementary functions”.
“Elementary” does not mean “easy”;
it is just the name for this class.
For example, how do we compute
the limit, as x approaches 1,
of this ugly-looking function?
As complex as the function may look,
it is in the class of functions
in the theorem, so it is continuous.
1 is certainly on its domain.
So to compute the limit,
I simply plug in x = 1.
That’s it. Nice!
An important warning:
the theorem ONLY applies
to these functions ON THEIR DOMAIN.
So NOT all limits
involving these functions
are a matter of “plug in the value.”
For example,
the function f(x) = sin(x)/x
is NOT continuous at x = 0,
because 0 is NOT in the domain.
If I wanted to compute
its limit as x approaches 0,
I could NOT simply evaluate.
Or the function tangent
is not continuous at x = π/2,

to understand what I am asking.
This feels a bit counterintuitive,
doesn't it?
If x is close to 0,
then f(x) is close to 0,
but f of IT is close to 1?
Surprisingly, it can be done.
And once you come up
with the right function,
it turns out to be a simple one.
If you do not know
how to solve this yet, do not worry.
Continue watching the video,
and afterwards come back to the problem.
It will be easier then.
There is a pseudo-theorem
that appears true intuitively,
but it is false. 
And that is what causes all the trouble.
Here it is.
IF I assume the limit as x approaches a
of f(x) is L
AND the limit
as y approaches L of g(y) is M
-- I am using y as the variable
because it will come in handy later;
do not worry about it --
THEN the limit as x approaches a
of g(f(x)) must be…
I want to say M
...but it isn’t.
This theorem is FALSE.
It is not a theorem; 
I cannot draw a conclusion in this case.
By contrast, if we care about continuity
rather than only about limits,
everything works fine.
I will call this Theorem 1,
and this one is true:
IF f is continuous at a
AND g is continuous at f(a),
THEN g composed with f
is continuous at a.
I will try to give you enough intuition
to understand why this happens,
why this is natural
rather than surprising.
After that I will offer 
two different ways
to fix the false theorem.
But first,
I want to present a counterexample
to fully convince you
that the false theorem
is, indeed, false.
Here is the counterexample:
I will define f(x)
to be x ⋅ sin(π/x) when x is not 0,
and I will define f(0) to be 0.
Because of the sin(π ÷ x),
this function oscillates wildly
as x approaches 0,
but it is sandwiched between x and -x
so it DOES have limit 0 
as x approaches 0;
it can be proven
with the Squeeze Theorem.
Next, I will define
the function g to be 2 at 0,
and 1 everywhere else.
It looks like a constant function,
with just a hole 
and the "wrong" value at 0.
This function ALSO has a limit at 0;
the limit is 1.
And yet, I claim
that for this example,
the limit at 0
of the composition of g and f
does NOT exist.
To understand why,
it is convenient to write
an explicit equation for g(f(x)).
It will be 2 for some values of x,
and 1 for others.
I am going to leave the rest
as an exercise;
it is very doable,
and I think it is a VERY USEFUL exercise.
If you work out yourself
when the composition is 2
and when it is 1
and then use it to conclude
that the limit does not exist,
it will clarify a lot what the problem
with composition of limits is,
and why the false theorem is false.
So go ahead and pause the video;
it will make the rest easier.
Back to comparing these two statements.
What I’ll do next is point
at the CORE of the problem.
There is a way to look at this problem
that helps gain intuition,
and I personally find it
much more enlightening
than writing a formal proof.
I have introduced this idea before: 
there is a key difference
between asking for a limit to exist,
and asking for a function 
to be continuous.
Roughly speaking, 
when I say that lim_(x → a) f(x) = L,
I mean that IF x is close to a BUT NOT a,
THEN f(x) is close to L.
By contrast, when I say
that f is continuous at a,
I mean that
IF x is close to a, INCLUDING a,
THEN f(x) is close to f(a).
Notice that, in a way,
the definition of continuity
has some symmetry
between the input and the output.
But the definition of limit doesn’t.
In a composition,
we are making the output of one function
be the input of another,
and this lack of symmetry
will cause trouble.
This observation
should help us understand
why one theorem is true,
and the other one is false.
Let’s look first
at the actual theorem, Theorem 1.
The first hypothesis roughly means
that IF x is close to a,
THEN f(x) is close to f(a).
The second hypothesis roughly means
that IF y is close to f(a),
THEN g(y) is close to g(f(a)).
Now, simply concatenate
the two implications:
IF x is close to a,
THEN f(x) is close to f(a),
and THEN g of IT
is close to g(f(a)),
Hey!
That is exactly 
what the conclusion means!
So the two hypotheses
imply the conclusion.
THAT wasn’t too bad.
By contrast,
let’s try to do the same thing
with the FALSE theorem,
and watch it fail and burn.
The first hypothesis roughly means
that IF x is close to a, but not a,
THEN f(x) is close to L.
The second hypothesis roughly means
that IF y is close to L,
BUT NOT L,
THEN g(y) is close to M.
And…
I cannot concatenate
these two implications!
Because the "then" part in the first
does NOT match 
the "if" part in the second.
THAT is why the theorem is false!
Okay. If we understood that,
let’s move to the last part of the video.
The theorem is false,
but can I fix it?
Can I still get a conclusion
about the limit of a composition
by using different hypotheses?
Yes!
There are two ways to fix the theorem.
We can either:
ask for a stronger hypothesis on f,
or ask for a stronger hypothesis on g.
I will call these new results
Theorems 2 and 3.
First: I could ask for g to have a limit,
just like before,
But then I request
something stronger for f:
I need f to have limit L,
and ALSO I want f(x) to NOT be L.
Think about it:
this is what was preventing us
from concatenating
the two implications earlier.
When I say f(x) is not L,
I need to specify FOR WHICH values of x.
I will say for all x
in an interval centered at a,
except maybe at a.
I could rewrite it
as mathematical symbols,
but I will leave it as words.
And with that extra condition, then yes,
I can conclude that the limit of g(f(x)),
as x approaches a, is M.
THAT is Theorem 2.
Alternatively,
I can keep the original hypothesis on f:
just asking that it has a limit,
and require a stronger hypothesis on g. 
If I ask for g to be continuous at L,
then I can also conclude
that the composition has a limit.
I will call THIS Theorem 3.
Now, I advise that you try to do
with these two theorems
what I did earlier with Theorem 1
and the False Theorem:
write the hypotheses as implications,
and see that they can be concatenated
to end up with the conclusion.
After you do that,
you will have a VERY clear understanding
of limit of compositions of functions,
of all the results that are
or are not true, and why.

In this video,
I will explain the types of discontinuity
a function may have,
and how some of them can be “removed”.
How may a function fail to be continuous?
Let’s remember the definition.
We say that a function f
is continuous at a point a
when the limit at a
is the same as the value f(a).
Otherwise, we say
that the function is discontinuous.
It is traditional
to classify discontinuities
as removable, and non-removable.
We have a removable discontinuity
when the limit EXISTS
but the limit is NOT equal
to the value of the function f at a.
And we have a non-removable discontinuity
when the limit of the function
does NOT exist.
I will explain
why this classification is useful,
and the reason for the name.
Here is an example
of a removable discontinuity.
The limit as x approaches a EXISTS,
but it is NOT equal to f(a).
In this case, the graph has a HOLE.
It does not matter
if the function is UNdefined at a,
or if it is defined
but with a different value.
In both cases, the graph HAS a hole,
and both cases count
as a removable discontinuity.
Why is it called removable?
Because it can be “fixed.” 
I will explain with an example.
Consider the function h,
defined by the equation h(x) = sin(x)/x.
As x approaches 0,
this function HAS limit 1,
but h(0) IS UNdefined.
This is what the graph looks like.
If this is the first time
you are encountering this function,
you may be surprised; 
it is not obvious that this limit is 1.
For now, just accept
that this is the case from the graph;
I will prove it in another video.
So the graph has a hole,
and the function has a discontinuity.
But I can remove it
by “filling" the hole!
What does this mean?
I am going to define a new function,
called H ("big H").
H is exactly the same 
as the original function when x is not 0.
But in addition, 
I am going to define H(0) to be 1.
When I do this,
H becomes CONTINUOUS at 0.
Think about the graph!
THIS is the graph
of the original function, h ("small h"),
with the hole at 0.
The new function H is exactly the same,
except it is also defined to be 1 at 0.
So the new function "fills the hole,"
so to speak, and THIS is the graph.
I am exaggerating a bit
how I fill the whole to make it clear;
I probably could simply draw the graph
like this.
What I did here,
“removing” the discontinuity,
is something we can ALWAYS do
as long as the limit exists.
And that is why we call it
a “removable” discontinuity.
In a way, it is as though
we had a continuous function,
but we forgot to define it at the point,
or we defined it with the "wrong" value.
In science,
and more particularly in physics,
it is common to remove discontinuities
automatically whenever possible.
Physicists normally
DON'T make any distinction
between a function
with a removable discontinuity,
and the same function
with the discontinuity removed.
For example,
if a physicist talks about sin(x)/x,
they are probably thinking about H;
about the "fixed" function.
By contrast, let’s look at some examples
of NON-removable discontinuities.
This function does NOT have a limit at 0,
because it has a JUMP.
It is a non-removable discontinuity;
try as we may,
we cannot “fix” this function;
we cannot make it continuous
by redefining it at 0.
This other function
does NOT have a limit at 1,
because it has a VERTICAL ASYMPTOTE at 1.
It CANNOT be "fixed," either;
it CANNOT be made continuous
by defining it at 1.
It ALSO has
a non-removable discontinuity.
And this final example
does not have a limit at 0, either.
It ALSO has
a non-removable discontinuity;
there is no way to fix it.
In short,
removable discontinuities can be...
well, removed!
Non-removable discontinuities CANNOT. 
THAT is the reason for the name.

In this video,

I evaluate at x = 2. And that's all
If the function is NOT continuous,
what do we do?
Try doing some algebraic manipulations
to convert the function
into a continuous one.
The manipulations
only need to be valid NEAR the point,
but NOT AT the point.
So for example, it is okay
to multiply and divide
by something that is 0 AT the point,
as long as it is not 0 NEAR the point.
For example, a common
and useful manipulation is FACTORING.
I want to compute this limit.
The function is continuous on its domain,
but 2 is NOT in the domain.
If I try to evaluate at 2, I get 0/0
This tells me that I can factor x - 2
in both the numerator and denominator.
And now I can divide both by x - 2.
The new function IS continuous at 2.

Here is a different type
of algebraic manipulation.
I want to compute this limit.
This function is NOT defined at 0;
if I try to evaluate at x = 0,
I get 0/0.
The numerator is giving me trouble
because of the square root;
I would like to get rid of it.
And I can do that, if I can multiply
the numerator and the denominator
by the conjugate.
Now in the numerator
I have difference times sum,
which is difference of squares.
And that will get rid
of the square root at the top.
I can now simplify the numerator,
then I divide both the numerator
and denominator by x.
And finally, the new function
is defined and continuous at 0.
To calculate the limit,
I simply evaluate at x = 0.
And that’s all!
The third method
is to try to transform the limit
into a different one
that you have already solved.

with an example.
It is a very specific one,
but it will clarify what I mean.

as x approaches 0 of sin(2x)/x.

of another one that I know.

the function whose limit is 1.

can be made more "interesting."

as in the previous example,

Higher order derivatives are
derivatives of derivatives.
There are two common systems of notation,
and it is useful to master both.
I will describe them in this video.
If f is a function, I can think of its
derivative f' as another function,
assuming f is differentiable,
at every point in the domain,
f' gives me another number.
And then I can also take the derivative
of the derivative,
which we call the second derivative.
And so on.
There are two standard systems of notation
for these higher order derivative.
First, there is Lagrange notation.
If I have a function f, its first
derivative, or simply its derivative,
is called f' (f prime).
Its second derivative is called f''
(f prime prime, or perhaps f double prime)
and so on.
But at some point we get tired of writing
primes.
For any positive integer n, I can define
the nth derivative of f,
and I write it as f, with an n in
parentheses as an exponent.
For example, consider the function f
defined by f(x) = x⁷.
Then the derivative is 7x⁶,
and the second derivative is 42x⁵,
the third derivative is 42·5, which is
210x⁴, and so on.
The 8th derivative would be 0, because
after 7 derivatives, all the x's are gone.
Be careful with this notation. The n in
the exponent needs to be in parentheses.
This way, the notation is standard, and
everybody uses it for the same purpose.
By contrast, if we write n as an exponent
of a function without the parentheses,
it is not clear what it means.
You have encountered this before
in some specific cases.
For trigonometric functions, it is
understood that
n as an exponent without parentheses
means power.
But this is for trigonometric
functions only.
There is a second usage, less common.
I have seen it more often with logarithms.
Here, an exponent without parentheses
could mean composition.
This is not universal,
so it is probably a good idea if we ever
want to use this notation to say so first.
But the notation for the derivative, with
the parentheses, is safe and unambiguous.
So do not forget those parentheses.
The second notation for higher order
derivatives is Leibniz notation.
This notation is particularly well suited
for physics.
Let's assume that y and x are physical
quantities, and that y is a function of x.
Then, as you know, we can write the first
derivative of this function as dy/dx.
But remember:
this does not mean that dy is a number
and dx is a number and I divide them.
Rather, here's how to interpret it:
start with a physical quantity y, and
apply the operator d/dx to it.
d/dx means take the derivative with
respect to x, and it is an operation.
When applied to the physical quantity y,
it produces a new physical quantity dy/dx,
and we call this the derivative of y
with respect to x.
With this same notation, how do we write
the second derivative? d²y/dx².
Why do we put the 2s in those places?
Think of it this way.
I start with a physical quantity y,
I apply the operator d/dx to it,
and then I apply the operator d/dx to it
again.
That is why I write the 2s in exactly
those places.
The 2 in the numerator tells us that
we perform 2 operations, 2 derivatives,
to the physical quantity y.
The 2 in the denominator tells us that we
took both derivatives with respect to x.
And we call this the second derivative of
y with respect to x.
More generally, how do I write the nth
derivative with this notation? dⁿy/dxⁿ.
Think of it as applying the operator d/dx
n times to the physical quantity y.
And we call it the nth derivative of y
with respect to x.
This notation has a particularly nice
advantage when we are dealing with
physical quantities.
It reminds us of the dimensions of the
derivatives.
By dimensions, I mean basically
their units.
For example, let's call x the position of
a particle, and let t represent time.
Assume that x depends on t.
The particle is moving.
We measure the position x in metres, 
and time t in seconds.
What about the derivatives?
The velocity is the derivative
of x with respect to time.
There is one x in the numerator,
given as metres,
and there is one t in the denominator,
given as seconds,
so we measure velocity in m/s.
Now look at the acceleration,
which is the second derivative of x
with respect to time.
There is one x in the numerator, but
TWO t's in the denominator.
The placement of those 2s
was very important.
So we measure the acceleration in m/s².
And that is a good advantage of the

We can keep track of the dimensions of

Chapter 4:
What is a function?
There are a few concepts related
to the notion of function...
...that can get confusing.
Strictly speaking,
they all have a very precise definition.
But in some contexts,
we relax those definitions,
we abuse notation,
and we use the terms
to mean something slightly different.
Sometimes students may learn
the same theorem...
...in two apparently contradictory ways,
depending on whether it is in a calculus
class, or in a linear algebra class.
But there is no contradiction if
we understand the conventions.
In this sequence of videos,
I will do two things:
first, I will explain the proper use of
these terms when we WANT to be strict.
Second,
I will explain the conventions used
in calculus to relax those definitions.
If you have taken a logic course,
perhaps you have learned
the definiton of function as:
"a subset of a cartesian product."
What I will write here is EQUIVALENT,
but in plainer English.
A function consists of three pieces:
first, a set of INPUTS called the DOMAIN.
In this example, the DOMAIN is the set A,
with elements 1, 2, and 3.
Second, a set of POTENTIAL OUTPUTS
called the CODOMAIN.
In this example, the CODOMAIN is the
set B, with elements 4, 5, and 6.
And third, a RULE that matches
each INPUT in the DOMAIN,
to exactly one OUTPUT in the CODOMAIN.
In the picture below, I am representing
the matches with arrows.
Notice the notation that I am using:
I am giving a NAME to the function,
in this case: "f".
Then, for each element "x" in the DOMAIN,
I am calling f of x (f(x)) the
corresponding element in the CODOMAIN.
So, the NAME of the function is "f",
NOT "f(x)".
f(x) is an element in the CODOMAIN,
NOT the NAME of the function.
Back to the example, there are only
three elements in this domain,
so I can just list all of the matches:
f(1) is 4, f(2) is 4, and f(3) is 6.
That is the RULE.
In most examples; however,
I will have an infinite domain,
and I will not be able to
list all of the matches.
Instead, I will probably describe the
RULE with an equation.
Why did I describe the elements in B
as POTENTIAL outputs?
I want to distinguish between
CODOMAIN, and RANGE.
The CODOMAIN is the set
of POTENTIAL outputs.
In this example, it is the set {4,5,6}.
The RANGE is the set of ACTUAL ouputs.
In this example,
it only has two elements: 4 and 6.
The CODOMAIN is a set
we determine beforehand,
from which we are allowed
to choose outputs.
And then, once we define the RULE,
we find out that we are only using
some of the outputs,
and those form the RANGE.
Notice that to define a function,
I only need to give the DOMAIN,
the CODOMAIN, and the RULE.
I do not need to explain
what the RANGE is.
The RANGE can be calculated
from the rest of the information.
Finally, one piece of notation.
If you read an expression like this:
f, colon, A, arrow, B (f: A → B),
it means that "f" is the NAME of a
function, A is the DOMAIN,
and B is the CODOMAIN.
It is just shorthand for all of that.
For example, 
I could define a function f,
with a DOMAIN of all the reals,
and a CODOMAIN of all the reals.
With the RULE given by the equation:
f(x) = 3 + x² + sin(x).
So far, I have explained the STRICT way
to define a function,
and to refer to its components.
Next, I will explain some conventions.
You may be thinking:
in calculus, or in pre-calculus,
I am used to defining functions in a much
easier way: just by giving the equation.
For example,
I may say the function g is defined by
g(x) = 1/(x^2), and say nothing else.
We do this all of the time.
Is it okay?
There is a convention that we use
in single-variable calculus.
We agree that it is okay to define
a function by just giving the RULE.
(normally as an equation)
And then, by default,
we assume that the domain is the
LARGEST SUBSET of the reals,
on which the RULE makes sense.
And we assume that the
codomain is all of the reals.
For example, when I define the function g
by the equation g(x) = 1/(x^2),
if I do not say anything else;
implicitly, I mean:
for which values of x does
this equation make sense?
I can divide by any non-zero number, so
x can be any real number EXCEPT for zero.
So that's what I mean.
The domain is all of the real numbers
EXCEPT for zero;
and I also imply that the codomain is ℝ.
This convention is useful,
because we are only talking about
this SPECIFIC type of function,
it saves a lot of time.
But once you move to other fields
of math, like abstract algebra,
where the domain and codomain may not
even be sets of numbers,
then you will have to be careful,
and specify domains and codomains
in order to define a function.
There is a second convention
worth mentioning.
Look at these two functions.
Are they the SAME function?
STRICTLY speaking,
they are NOT the same.
They have the same domain, and
the same rule, even the same range,
but they have DIFFERENT codomains.
They differ by the element "5".
In practice, particularly in calculus,
we often abuse notation and PRETEND that
these two functions ARE the same function,
and we use the same name for both.
Effectively,
we treat the codomain as irrelevant,
and we only pay attention to the range.
If you had never heard the word
“codomain” before, this may be why.
I will talk more about this
abuse of notation in a later video...
...about SURJECTIVE functions.
But remember that
in other areas of mathematics,
this distinction may be important,
and in that case,
we will stick to saying
that these two functions are DIFFERENT.
To make matters worse,
this notation is not universal!
The names I have introduced here are
the most commonly used names in math.
But sometimes people use different names,
at least in some areas
of computer science.
They still use the word DOMAIN
in the same way that I have.
But what I have called the CODOMAIN,
they call the RANGE,
and what I have called the RANGE,
they call the IMAGE.
This makes it confusing,
because now RANGE may mean
two different things,
depending on who you ask;
but don’t blame me!
The moral is,
that if you are working
