is the positive reals,
which is the range of the exponential,
as it should be.
Notice that one graph is
the REFLECTION of the other
with respect to the diagonal
of the first and third quadrants.
This always happens with
pairs of inverse functions.
Switching from a function to its inverse,
means swapping the roles of x and y.
Now think of swapping the x axis
and the y axis:
it is the same as reflecting
with respect to the diagonal.
That’s why graphs of pairs of inverse
functions always have this property.
Finally, the composition of a function
and its inverse should be the identity.
Indeed,
ln(eˣ) is x,
FOR ALL real numbers x.
And eˡⁿ⁽ʸ⁾ is y,
FOR ALL POSITIVE reals y.
As a last example, I will look
at something more interesting.
Consider the function f defined by:
f(x) = x².
Here is its graph.
We can’t quite find an inverse for it.
This function is NOT one-to-one.
For example, f(3) = f(-3). Therefore,
it does NOT have an inverse function.
However, we can still try to construct
something CLOSE to an inverse.
Here is the graph again.
Instead of the full function,
I could RESTRICT to a smaller domain
and consider only a “piece” of it.
For example,
I could keep just the function
represented by this piece of the graph,
in RED.
Then, I am maintaining the FULL range,
with a SMALLER domain,
and now the new function IS one-to-one,
so it will have an inverse!
To be clear, this is not
the only choice I could have made.
I could have chosen this
other half of the graph instead.
Or, if I get creative, perhaps this:
but that would be silly.
Instead,
let’s stick to my original choice.
What is the inverse...
...of the function represented by
the piece of the graph in red?
It is the square root!
Indeed,
let us call g the function that is
defined by the equation g(x) = √x.
g is NOT the inverse of the function f.
g is the inverse of the restriction of
the function f to the domain [0,∞).
This is actually how you learned of
square roots back in primary school,
even though you did not use these words.
For example,
you learned that there are two real
numbers whose square is 9: 3 and -3,
but that when we write √9, we mean
the positive "3".
So yes, the square root is the inverse of
the RESTRICTION of f to a smaller domain.
Equivalently, saying that a number "a",
is the square root of a number "b",
means that b is a²...
(if I were to stop here,
it would mean that f and g
were inverses of each other,
which they are not)
...and also that "a" is positive or zero.
But there is more!
We know that when we compose a
function and its inverse,
we should get the identity.
This will still be true,
as long as I stay in the right domain.
What is the √(x²)?
It is x, but only if x was positive
or zero to begin with;
otherwise, as you know,
it is the absolute value of x (|x|).
The moral is, if we have
a function that is NOT one-to-one,
we can restrict it to a smaller domain
and CONSTRUCT its inverse.
But the way we define inverse,
and the properties of inverse functions,
will have to be modified accordingly.
This will become very important when we
define inverse trigonometric functions.

In this video,
I want to explore the relation between
the derivative of a function,
and the derivative of its inverse.
I want to answer this question:
if a function is differentiable,
and it has an inverse,
must the inverse function
also be differentiable?
In other words, if f has a derivative,
must f⁻¹ have a derivative as well?
Moreover,
Can I write (f⁻¹)' in terms of f'?
To the first question the answer is:
not necessarily,
unless we add an extra condition.
I can explain it geometrically.
Consider as an example the function f
described by this graph, in blue.
This function is differentiable:
it has a derivative everywhere.
It is also one-to-one
and it has an inverse.
As you know, we can obtain the graph of
its inverse by reflecting the graph of f
with respect to the diagonal
of the first and third quadrants.
Here is the graph of the inverse, in red.
Now, if the graph in blue has a
well-defined tangent line at every point,
then the graph in red,
which is a reflection,
will also have a well-defined
tangent line at every point?
So, f⁻¹ should be differentiable
everywhere, right?
Not quite.
Consider the point I am labelling P
in the graph of f.
At that point, the tangent line is
horizontal, and the derivative is 0.
I am labelling Q the corresponding
point for the inverse.
Think about what happens when we reflect
a horizontal tangent line:
f⁻¹ must have a vertical tangent line
at Q, with infinite slope.
And thus, f⁻¹ is not differentiable at
Q.
Therefore, it is possible for f to have
a derivative while f⁻¹ does NOT.
But this is the only thing
that may go wrong.
We get a nice theorem.
Consider a function f that is
defined on an interval I:
IF:
f has an inverse,
f is differentiable,
and the derivative f’ is never 0.
THEN:
we can conclude that f⁻¹
is also differentiable.
Side note:
this little result should not be confused
with its “big brother” so-to-speak.
There is another theorem called the
Inverse Function Theorem.
The Inverse Function Theorem
is much more complicated,
and much more important as well.
It belongs in a
multivariable calculus course,
so it is beyond the scope of
this sequence of videos.
But just in case you have heard of the
Inverse function Theorem:
that is not what I am talking about.
The little result I am presenting
here is much less ambitious.
Back to what I was doing:
once we know that both f and f⁻¹
are differentiable,
how can I write the derivative of f⁻¹
in terms of the derivative of f?
We already know
a relation between f and f⁻¹:
f(f⁻¹(y)) = y,
FOR ALL y in some interval.
(I am being careless with domains.)
Then, if I take the
derivative of both sides,
I will get a relation between the
derivative of f
and the derivative of f⁻¹.
Let’s do that!
The derivative of the left equals
the derivative of the right.
On the left I need to use the chain rule:
I get f'(f⁻¹(y))⋅(f⁻¹)'(y).
And on the right, I simply get "1".
This is promising,
but this equation looks more complicated
than it actually is.
I can make it look nicer:
if I call f⁻¹(y): "x",
or equivalently, I set y = f(x),
then the equation says that
f'(x)⋅(f⁻¹)'(y) is 1.
And from here I can solve:
(f⁻¹)'(y) is 1/(f'(x)).
I just have to remember that f and f⁻¹
are evaluated at DIFFERENT points!
And that’s the equation
I was looking for!

In this video,
I will try to obtain a formula
for the derivative of an exponential
from the definition of
derivative as a limit.
In the process, I will come up with
a defining property of the number "e".
There are many exponential functions,
one for each base.
The base should be a positive number "a".
If "a" is greater than 1,
then aˣ is increasing.
If "a" is between 0 and 1,
then aˣ is decreasing.
Either way,
the domain is all of the reals,
and the range is the positive reals.
Let’s fix a positive number "a",
and define the function f as:
f(x) = aˣ.
What is its derivative f’(x)?
First, I write the definition
of derivative as a limit.
Then I use the definition of f,
both at x+h, and at x.
I can use a property of exponentials
to write aˣ⁺ʰ as: aˣ⋅aʰ.
And now I can take aˣ as a common factor.
Let’s pause for a moment.
For the purpose of the limit,
the variable is "h".
This term, aˣ, does not depend on h,
so for the purpose of the limit,
it is a just a CONSTANT,
and I can take it out of the limit.
Look at what happened:
the limit no longer depends on x.
It still depends on a, but ONLY on a.
Let’s give it a name:
I am going to call this limit "Lₐ".
So far, I have concluded that the
derivative of aˣ is: (a number)⋅aˣ.
So the derivative of an arbitrary
exponential is a MUTLIPLE OF ITSELF,
and the multiplicity constant
depends on the base "a".
I do not know the value
of the constant in general,
but the value of a for which
this constant is 1,
whatever that value of a is,
will be very important.
Let’s give it a name.
We define "e" to be the ONLY number
for which this limit is 1.
Or, equivalently, e is the only
number that satisfies that:
the derivative of eˣ,
with respect to x, is itself.
So far, I have pretended that
everything was working fine,
but if we want to be rigorous, there
are a few issues with this derivation.
First, I have been assuming this limit
exists, but I have not proven it.
A priori, this limit might not exist,
and then the function
would be non-differentiable.
Second, even if this limit exists
FOR ALL positive numbers "a",
how do I know there is one value of a
for which the limit is 1?
I would need to prove that.
And third, if I am being really rigorous,
there is an even bigger problem:
I have been assuming that exponential
functions are always well-defined,
but I did not explain how.
This might be a surprise,
but it is not obvious how to define aˣ
when x is an arbitrary real number;
and I should take care of that before
I even try to compute the derivative.
I will explain this problem
in the next video.
Nevertheless, despite all those caveats,
I still find this derivation helpful.
I think it helps understand
where the number "e" comes from,
and why exponentials have the
derivative that they have.
If you care about making this much more
rigorous, watch the next video.
(link in the description)
But if you find this explanation
to be enough, you may skip it.

In this video,
I will review the definition of
exponentials and logarithms,
and I will explain why they are
difficult to define rigorously.
It may surprise you
to learn that this is...
...MUCH harder than they
told you in high school.
To be fair, for most practical purposes,
this is not something you
have to worry about too much.
But I find it interesting to at least
KNOW that this IS a difficult problem.
First,
let’s recall the definition of logarithms
that you learned in high school.
When I say that logₐ(x) is a number "y",
it means that: aʸ = x.
For example,
if I want to compute log₂(8),
I am asking: which power of 2 = 8?
I can just guess: it is 3.
So log₂(8) = 3.
That’s it.
This definition is simply saying that
the logarithm in base "a",
is the INVERSE function of
the exponential with base "a".
Nothing more.
We can use this to
define logarithm functions,
AS LONG AS the exponential
functions are well-defined.
So let’s review how we
define exponentials, or "powers".
I want to define "aᶜ", where the
exponent, "c", is any real number.
But the base, "a",
will only be a positive number.
With a negative base, some exponents
still make sense, but not all of them.
For example, (-1)¹ᐟ³ is ³√(-1),
which is -1.
But (-1)¹ᐟ² is √(-1),
which is undefined.
So, we will stick with
positive bases only.
Let’s start with rational exponents.
We can break this into cases.
First, the easiest case:
the exponent is a positive integer.
I am denoting the
positive integers as: ℤ⁺.
When n is a positive integer, aⁿ is
defined as iterated multiplication.
You learned this in grade 1 or 2.
Second, we define a¹ᐟⁿ,
(for example: a¹ᐟ², a¹ᐟ³, and so on...)
as an "n-th root". (ⁿ√a)
And ⁿ√a is defined implicitly
by saying that its n-th power is a.
Since we had already defined
"n-th power" in Step 1,
this is a valid way
to define "n-th root".
Third,
for an arbitrary positive rational n/m,
we can define aⁿᐟᵐ,
as first "aⁿ",
and then the result of that to the 1/m.
Notice that we are using
Steps 1 and 2 in Step 3.
So far, this has defined a raised to
any POSITIVE rational exponent.
We define a⁰ as 1.
And finally, we know how to define
a power with a negative exponent,
in terms of a power
with a positive exponent.
That's it! So far there are no problems.
We have defined any power with
a positive base and a rational exponent.
But how do we define a power
when the exponent is NOT rational?
For example, what is 2 ⷧ?
If you have never thought about it,
this may come as a surprise.
I cannot define it in terms of iterated
multiplication, or roots, or quotients.
It seems there is no easy way 
to define 2 ⷧ.
There is no way around it.
We need to get into serious analysis
territory to even say what 2 ⷧ means.
Here is one possible solution.
We know π has a decimal expansion,
which is infinite and is not periodic.
That is why π is irrational.
I do not know how to compute 2 ⷧ,
but I can compute 2 to the 3,
I can compute 2 to the 3.1,
because 3.1 is rational,
I can compute 2 to the 3.14,
because 3.14 is rational,
I can compute 2 to the 3.141,
and so on...
This way I can construct
a sequence of numbers,
and I can declare 2 ⷧ to be
the limit of this sequence.
This is probably the most natural way
to explain what 2 ⷧ means.
It is also probably what early
mathematicians were implicitly assuming
before they started giving
this problem some serious thought.
But this solution is messy.
How do I know that
this sequence has a limit?
And even if I do,
there is a bigger problem.
Effectively, I am approximating π
with a sequence of rational numbers.
But what if I use a DIFFERENT sequence
of rational numbers to approximate π,
will I get the same result?
The answer turns out to be yes,
but it is VERY HARD to prove.
This solution may look simple,
but it isn’t.
There are better ways.
The key is to use this identity:
I can write aᶜ as eᶜˡⁿ⁽ᵃ⁾.
How can this be helpful?
I am writing an exponential
with an arbitrary base a,
in terms of an exponential with base e,
so I have simplified
the problem a little bit.
I only need to worry about defining
exponentials with base "e" rigorously,
and other bases will follow.
This leads to what I call
the Modern Analysis Solution.
It goes in three steps.
Step 1:
define one single function from scratch,
either the exponential with base e
or the natural logarithm.
Of course, this is the difficult step.
I will come back to it.
Step 2:
define the other function as its inverse.
So, if we have managed to define the
exponential with base e from scratch,
define the natural logarithm
as its inverse,
and if we have managed to define
the natural logarithm from scratch,
define the exponential with base e
as its inverse.
And Step 3: define other
exponentials with this equation.
Ok, then. How do we do Step 1?
I have seen at least
three different options,
used in different areas of analysis.
The first option is
to use differential equations.
The function eˣ is the only function that
is its own derivative,
and that has value 1 at 0.
This is called an “Initial Value Problem”,
which appears often in the field
of differential equations.
There is a result,
the Picard-Lindelöf theorem,
that says that a problem like this
has exactly one solution.
In other words, that it is okay
to define a function this way.
The advantage of this option
is that it is very natural.
It is easy to understand.
We recognize this as the most important
property of the number e.
The disadvantage,
is that the Picard-Lindelöf theorem
is very expensive.
It is a very sophisticated theorem,
and it is hard to prove.
You would learn it in a course
in ordinary differential equations.
This counts as advanced math.
Another option is to define eˣ
as a power series.
Specifically,
to define eˣ with this equation.
You would need the full theory of
power series, not just a single theorem.
You normally learn it when you study
Taylor series...
...at the end of a calculus course.
A disadvantage of this option,
is that proving the properties of the
exponential from this definition,
is harder.
An advantage of this option,
is that it generalizes very easily if
we want to use complex numbers,
rather than only real numbers.
And finally, my favourite option:
using integrals.
Instead of defining the
exponential from scratch,
we can define the natural logarithm
from scratch.
It may feel perverse
to define ln(x) before eˣ,
but there is nothing wrong with it.
We can define ln(x),
as the integral from 1 to x of (1/t)⋅dt.
( ∫₁ˣ((1/t)⋅dt) )
To show that this is well-defined,
and to prove all of its properties,
we only need the
Fundamental Theorem of Calculus,
which you will normally learn
in any calculus course.
Of all the options, this is
by far the most accessible.
A good calculus student
COULD fill in the details.
To wrap things up.
My goal in this video was not to present
the full rigorous construction,
proving all the details.
Rather, as I said at the beginning,
my goal was to explain why defining
exponentials rigorously is difficult.
I think every calculus student should
understand why this is not trivial.
But if you are curious for more,
if you would like to learn the details
and to complete the construction,
then I invite you to consider
taking an analysis class.

What is the derivative of
the natural logarithm?
The goal of this video is to obtain a
formula for the derivative of ln(x).
We already know that
the derivative of eˣ is itself.
But how can this help me?
Exponentials and logarithms
are INVERSES of each other,
so I know that eˡⁿ⁽ˣ⁾ = x,
FOR ALL positive numbers x.
This is helpful because it is a relation
between the two functions.
If I now take the
derivative of both sides,
I will get a relation between
the derivative of the exponential,
and the derivative of the logarithm.
These two expressions are equal, so
their derivatives must be equal as well.
On the left-hand side,
I have the derivative of e
to the power of a function.
By chain rule,
that will be itself, multiplied by
the derivative of the exponent.
And on the right-hand side,
I simply get "1".
Great! Look--this is
the piece I am looking for.
Before solving for it,
I can re-write eˡⁿ⁽ˣ⁾ as "x".
And finally,
I solve for the piece I want.
The derivative of ln(x),
with respect to x, = 1/x,
and that’s my final answer.
Notice that this same trick can be used
to find the derivative of ANY function,
IF we know the derivative of
its INVERSE function first.

We know that the derivative
of eˣ is itself,
but what is the derivative of an
exponential with a DIFFERENT base?
That is the goal of this video.
The base "a" could be
any positive real number,
it has to be positive, otherwise the
exponential does not make sense.
I will use a trick
that is very common in mathematics.
I want to solve the general problem
FOR ALL values of a,
and I already know the answer
for one value: "e".
I am going to try to write
the general case (FOR ALL a),
in terms of the one case I can solve.
So, write aˣ in terms of eˣ.
And here is how to do it:
any positive number "a", can be
written as eˡⁿ⁽ᵃ⁾.
(This is by definition of ln.)
Then aˣ, can be written as (eˡⁿ⁽ᵃ⁾)ˣ.
And now I can use
the properties of powers.
(And perhaps it is more common
to write this as: eˣˡⁿ⁽ᵃ⁾.)
And that's all I need!
I have written aˣ in terms of eˣ.
That's going to allow me
to take the derivative.
So now, the derivative of aˣ is...
Instead of as aˣ,
I will think of it as eˣˡⁿ⁽ᵃ⁾.
I know the derivative of eˣ is itself,
so the derivative of e to a function,
by chain rule,
will be itself, multiplied by
the derivative of the exponent.
And now that I have taken the derivative,
I can re-write this
as it was originally (as aˣ).
And here I have x multiplied by a number,
so the derivative is just
the multiplying number: ln(a).
And that's my final answer!
The derivative of an
arbitrary exponential is itself,
multiplied by ln of the base.
Notice that when a = e,
I get ln(e), which is 1.
So this term disappears, and I recover
the identity that I already knew.
This strategy,
reducing a bigger problem to a
smaller problem we have already solved,
is very common in mathematics.
Here is an exercise
for you to practice it.
You already know that
the derivative of ln(x) is 1/x,
but what is the derivative of logarithm
on a DIFFERENT base?
As a hint, use the logarithm
change of base identity.

In this video, I will introduce the
method of logarithmic differentiation.
We use this to take
the derivative of a function,
that is raised to the power
of another function.
I will illustrate logarithmic
differentiation with an example.
We consider this function f, defined by:
f(x) = (cos(x))ˢᶦⁿ⁽ˣ⁾,
and I want to take its derivative.
The first thing to notice,
is that this function is NOT
merely a power or an exponential.
What do I mean by that?
A power would be a function that
depends on x only in the base,
and the exponent is a number.
For example, x³,
and I know how to take its derivative:
that's what the power rule tells me.
I could also have a power composed
with a different function,
for example: cos³(x),
which is simply (cos(x))³.
I know how to take the derivative
of this function as well:
I just combine the power rule
with the chain rule.
On the other hand,
an exponential would be a function
that depends on x in the exponent,
but the base is a constant (is a number),
for example: 2ˣ.
Again, I have a formula to take
derivatives of functions like this:
I know the derivative of 2ˣ is 2ˣ⋅ln(2).
And again, I could have a function like
this composed with something else,
for example: 2ˢᶦⁿ⁽ˣ⁾.
To take its derivative, I would use
this rule, together with chain rule.
The problem with my original function is
that it isn't any one of those.
Neither the base nor the exponent is a
constant, they BOTH depend on x.
So, none of these rules
(by itself) tell me what to do.
I am going to present two methods
to take care of this,
which are actually
the SAME method in disguise.
The first method consists of
using this identity,
which allows me to re-write
any power exponential,
as an exponential with base "e".
Why is this useful?
Because I know how to take the derivative
of an exponential with base e!
In our case, this function
can be re-written as: eˢᶦⁿ⁽ˣ⁾⋅ˡⁿ⁽ᶜᵒˢ⁽ˣ⁾⁾.
And once I have it this way,
I know how to take the derivative:
this is an exponential of a function.
So, the derivative will be itself,
times the derivative of the exponent.
And now, I can re-write this
as it was originally.
And to take this derivative,
I will use the product rule.
And that's it!
Perhaps we can simplify it a little bit,
but that was the first method.
The second method is the one we call
"logarithmic differentiation."
Here is the function whose
derivative I want to take.
The difficulty is in having this exponent
here; so, before I do anything,
I'm going to begin by taking
logarithms of both sides.
This is a good idea because logarithms
simplify many operations.
They turn products into sums,
quotients into differences,
and powers into products.
So, using the properties of logarithms,
I can re-write this as:
ln(f(x)) = (sin(x))⋅ln(cos(x)).
And look,
the exponent has disappeared entirely!
Now that the exponent is gone,
I can take derivatives of
both sides much more easily.
On the left-hand side,
I no longer have just "f", I have ln(f).
But, I can take the derivative
using implicit differentiation.
And on the right-hand side,
I have to take the derivative of this
product, so just use the product rule.
If this feels familiar,
it's not an accident!
It's exactly the same derivative that
I computed in method one.
And finally, I can solve for f'(x),
which is what I wanted.
And that's it!
The answer is the same as in method one.

In this video, I want to write a proof
of the power rule for derivatives,
and I want the proof
to work for ANY REAL exponent.
The power rule states
that the derivative of xᶜ,
with respect to x,
is c⋅xᶜ⁻¹, where c is a constant.
In an earlier video,
(link in the description)
I already wrote a proof for
when c is a positive integer,
but the result is true
for any REAL constant c.
The key to proving the power rule
in general is to use this identity:
for any real number c,
and for any positive number x,
xᶜ is defined as eᶜˡⁿ⁽ˣ⁾.
It may feel overkill to use logarithms
in order to prove the power rule.
Aren’t powers more
“basic” than logarithms?
Shouldn’t we prove the power rule
BEFORE we introduce logarithms?
Well... it depends:
if we are happy to stick only
to RATIONAL exponents c,
then there are alternative proofs for
the power rule, without using logarithms.
But if we want to prove the power rule
for REAL exponents,
there is no way around it.
This identity is not merely a property
of logarithms and exponentials;
it is THE definition of a power
with a real exponent.
I will add a link in the description
to another video...
...where I discuss this
in a bit more detail.
The good news is that
once we have this identity,
the proof of the power rule
is surprisingly quick,
as long as we already know the
derivatives of eˣ and ln(x).
Here it is.
I want to compute the derivative of xᶜ.
First, I re-write xᶜ as eᶜˡⁿ⁽ˣ⁾.
Now I have to take the derivative
of the exponential of a function.
Using chain rule, it will be itself
times the derivative of the exponent.
Next, I can re-write eᶜˡⁿ⁽ˣ⁾ as xᶜ,
and the derivative of c⋅ln(x) is c÷x.
Finally, I regroup xᶜ÷x as xᶜ⁻¹,
and that’s it!
That completes the proof.
Notice that this proof works
for all exponents at once:
natural, rational, real,
and even complex!
We paid a high price for it though:
we needed to develop
the theory of logarithms first,
and know their properties.
And we also needed to prove the formula
for the derivatives of eˣ and ln(x),
BEFORE proving this version
of the power rule.
To be fair,
this proof only LOOKS short
because we had to spend effort beforehand
on developing more sophisticated tools.

Ln or log:
what’s the controversy?
There are some SPECIAL BASES
for logarithms,
but we can’t agree on what to call them.
If I have a favourite base, call it: "b",
then I can write exponentials 
with any other base,
in terms of exponentials with base b.
And I can write logarithms 
with any other base,
in terms of logarithms with base b.
So why don’t we find the BEST BASE 
and use it always as the only one?
Mathematicians soon realized that
the base "e" was special,
and they gave a special name to
logarithms with base e: "ln".
(Sometimes read as “lawn”)
This is called the natural logarithm,
although I like to think the "n"
stands for John Napier: 
the inventor of logarithms.
What’s special about e?
Exponentials with base e 
appear naturally as limits.
Some limits involving eˣ, or ln(x),
have nice clean values,
whereas the same limits with 
other bases... not so much.
eˣ and ln(x) have SIMPLER derivatives
than exponentials or logarithms
with any other bases.
eˣ and ln(x) also have beautiful
power series expansions.
The evidence is plenty.
Natural logarithm is natural, and it is
the only logarithm you should ever use.
But wait! Some people disagree.
To understand why,
we have to go back to a time before
computers, or even calculators,
back when everything had to
be computed by hand,
with the help of log tables, 
and slide rules.
Since we use the decimal system 
to write numbers,
the logarithm in base 10,
(sometimes called the "common logarithm"
or "decimal logarithm")
was the most useful 
to do calculations by hand.
It became common to write "log", without
specifying the base, to mean base 10.
Fast forward to the present.
Nobody computes by hand anymore,
and slide rules are museum relics.
There is no longer the same need
for logarithms in base 10.
Modern mathematicians say hey!
"log" by itself should clearly be 
reserved for the most important base,
the only one that matters: "e".
But it is hard to change tradition,
and engineers have never allowed...
...mathematicians to tell them
how to use notation,
so they continue using "ln" instead.
And that is the situation today.
Whenever you see ln(x) OR log(x), they 
both probably mean logarithm in base e.
When you read a book, 
or do math with somebody,
it is probably a good idea to find out
which convention they use.
ln(x) is most commonly used in science
and engineering, but also in calculus!
Probably because most calculus students
are in science and engineering,
rather than math.
log(x) is most commonly used in math.
If you take any math courses
beyond calculus,
they will probably use log to
refer to the natural logarithm.
Which notation do YOU prefer?

In this video,
I will define the function ARCSINE,
and I will explain 
how to compute with it.
Some books, and most calculators,
refer to this function as "SINE INVERSE."
I prefer not to do that.
ARCSINE is NOT the 
inverse function of sine
It is a bit more subtle.
This is the graph of SINE.
As you know,
the domain is all of the reals,
and the range
is the interval from -1 to 1.
However,
this is not a one-to-one function.
For example, sin(0) = sin(π).
Two different inputs 
produce the same output.
Sine is not one-to-one,
and therefore it 
does not have an inverse function.
That’s it.
That is why I prefer the name "arcsine",
rather than "sine inverse."
So, how do we define arcsine if it
isn't just the inverse?
Sine is not one-to-one,
but we can restrict it
to a smaller domain,
hopefully preserving the full range,
so that it becomes one-to-one.
For example,
we can keep just the part of the function
defined by this piece of the graph,
in red.
The restriction would then be one-to-one.
Notice that this is an arbitrary choice. 
Instead of this piece,
I could have chosen this piece,
or this other one,
or many others,
they are all equally good.
Any of those restrictions is 
one-to-one and has an inverse.
Since we do not have a reason
to prefer any of them,
it is common to choose the one with
the domain symmetric around 0,
the one in the graph here.
We give a special name to its inverse:
we call it arcsine.
But again,
this is just a convention,
and any of the other choices could
serve a similar purpose.
And that’s the definition of arcsine!
It is the inverse function of:
the RESTRICTION of sine
to the interval [-π/2,π/2].
NOT merely the inverse function itself!
Here is the graph of
the restriction of sine.
Arcsine is its inverse,
so this is its graph:
it is the reflection
of the previous one,
with respect to the diagonal
of the first quadrant.
And here are the two graphs
on top of each other.
The domain of the
restriction of sine is [-π/2,π/2],
which becomes the range of arcsine,
and the range of sine is [-1,1],
which becomes the domain of arcsine.
Here is a second way to state
exactly the same thing:
that the two functions are
inverses of each other.
x = arcsin(y) is equivalent
to y = sin(x),
as long as x and y
are in the right intervals.
x, the argument of sine, must be
between -π/2 and π/2,
and y, the argument of arcsine,
must be between -1 and 1.
This statement contains
all of the information you could
ever need about arcsine.
Any property of arcsine
will follow from this definition,
as long as you know
the sine function well.
For example,
what is arcsin(1/2)?
By definition,
if I call arcsin(1/2) "t",
this means that sin(t) is 1/2,
and that t must be between
-π/2 and π/2.
Now use your knowledge of trigonometry,
and draw a picture if needed.
There are many angles with a sine of 1/2,
but only one in this region: π/6.
And that’s the answer!
Here is yet a third equivalent way
to write this definition.
If we compose sine and arcsine,
we should get the identity,
(because they are inverses of each other)
but ONLY on the correct intervals.
So, arcsin(sin(x)) will always be x,
as long as x is between -π/2 and π/2,
and sin(arcsin(y)) will always be y,
as long as y is between -1 and 1.
Be careful. These identities are not true
for numbers OUTSIDE of these intervals.
For example, what is sin(arcsin(2))?
It is undefined!
2 is not in the domain of arcsine.
arcsin(2) is not a real number.
But what about arcsin(sin(2))?
This IS well defined.
This IS a number.
But I cannot use the identity above!
The answer is NOT 2.
How do I solve this problem then?
There are a few ways. Here is one.
Think of sin(2) simply as a number.
We know that, by definition,
arcsine of a number = t is equivalent to
asking that sin(t) = that number,
with the extra requirement that
t must be between -π/2 and π/2.
That’s all.
So I have reduced this to
a trigonometric question.
I am trying to find a number t,
between -π/2 and π/2,
which has the same sine as 2.
Now, use any trigonometry you like
to solve this problem:
maybe trigonometric identities,
or look at the unit circle,
or observe the graph of sine...
I will try to solve it by looking
at the graph of sine.
Here in red is a sketch
of the graph of y = sin(x).
I am looking for a number t
which is between -π/2 and π/2,
(so it should be somewhere here)
whose sine is equal to sin(2).
Let’s find sin(2) on the graph.
2 is somewhere between π/2 and π.
Perhaps 2 is here,
and then sin(2) is this y value.
The value t that I am looking for
must be between -π/2 and π/2,
and have the same sine value as 2.
That means it must be exactly here.
That’s it.
Can we write an equation for t?
Looking at the symmetry of the graph,
I think that this distance,
must be the same as this distance.
The first distance is (π/2) - t,
and the second distance is 2 - (π/2).
I can solve for t,
and I get that t = π - 2.
And that’s the answer!
I have proven that
arcsin(sin(2)) is π - 2.
Here is a challenge for you:
what is arcsin(sin(6))?
This is a very good exercise...
...to test whether you have
understood the definition of arcsine.
I will give you a hint:
the answer is neither 6,
nor π - 6.

In this video I will obtain an equation
for the derivative of arcsin.
I have already introduced
the function...
...and explained how to compute with it
in the previous video,
which I will link in the description.
I am going to use a standard trick.
I know the derivative of sin, and I want
to obtain the derivative of arcsin.
Since, in the right domains,
they are inverses of each other,
I know that they are related
by this equation.
If I take the derivative of
both sides of the equation,
I will get a new identity that will
relate the derivative of arcsin
to the derivative of sin.
That’s it.
That’s my plan.
The rest is calculations.
Let’s do it then!
The derivative of the left should be
equal to the derivative of the right.
On the left I have the derivative
of sin of a function.
Using the chain rule,
that is cos of the argument multiplied
by the derivative of the argument.
And on the right, I get 1.
From here I can solve,
and the derivative of arcsin(x) is
1/(cos(arcsin(x))).
I could perhaps leave it as is,
but I can re-write it in a nicer way.
What is cos(arcsin(x))?
For simplicity, so as not to write
arcsin(x) all of the time,
I will give it a name.
I will call it θ.
Then, sin(θ) = x,
and I am looking for cos(θ).
Once I put it this way, I see
that this is a problem in trigonometry.
I know sin of the angle θ, and I want
to write cos of the same angle θ.
There are various ways to do this:
with trig identities,
drawing right triangles,
looking at the unit circle...
I am going to use a trig identity.
I know that cos²(θ) = 1 - sin²(θ);
therefore, cos(θ) is ±√(1 - sin²(θ)),
or simply ±√(1 - x²).
But which one is it?
Plus or minus?
This is a well-defined function.
It can only have one value (one sign),
and I cannot choose + for convenience
and pretend to be done.
I need to justify the sign that I choose.
The key is in the definition of arcsin.
We defined arcsin as the inverse of a
SPECIFIC RESTRICTION of sin:
θ must be between -π/2 and π/2;
and in this region,
(in the first and fourth quadrants),
cos(θ) is always positive or zero.
That’s what I needed to know.
I choose the sign +.
Notice that if I had defined arcsin...
...as the inverse of
any other restriction of sin,
then I might be getting
a different sign here.
Now I can go back to
the original calculation,
and I conclude that the derivative of
arcsin(x), with respect to x,
is 1/√(1 - x²).
And that’s the final answer!

In the previous two videos,
I defined the function arcsin,
I explained how to compute with it,
and I found its derivative.
In this video we will do the same thing
for the functions arctan and arccos.
I assume you have watched
the previous videos already,
so this time I will go faster.
First, let’s remember what
we learned about arcsin.
The function sin is not one-to-one,
so it does not have an inverse.
But we can restrict it to a smaller
domain to make it one-to-one.
We keep only the function represented by
this piece of the graph in red,
and then it is one-to-one.
We define arcsin as its inverse:
as the inverse of the restriction of sin
to the interval [-π/2,π/2].
This is the graph of the restriction
of sin, in blue.
And in red we have the graph of
its inverse: arcsin.
These are the two graphs together.
We can also summarize
that the functions are inverses of each
other with the following statement:
x = arcsin(y) IF AND ONLY IF y = sin(x),
as long as x and y are
in the right intervals.
(This is for all x between [-π/2,π/2]
and for all y between [-1,1].)
If this went too fast, I invite you
to watch the previous two videos.
Ok then! Let’s try to do
the same thing for tangent.
Here is the graph of tangent.
Notice that the domain is NOT
all of the reals.
tan is not defined
at any odd multiple of π/2.
Like sin, tan is not one-to-one,
and it does not have an inverse.
Like we did for sin, we can reduce
the domain to make it one-to-one.
It is standard to consider the function
defined by just this piece of the graph,
in red.
Then, it is one-to-one,
and we call its inverse ARCTANGENT.
This is to say,
we define arctan as the inverse of
the RESTRICTION of tan to (-π/2,π/2).
By contrast, remember that we used the
CLOSED interval to define arcsin.
Here is the graph, in blue,
of the restriction of tan.
And here is the graph, in red,
of its inverse arctan.
Here is both of them together.
Since they are inverses of each other,
we can obtain one by reflecting the other
with respect to the diagonal
of the first and third quadrants.
And just like we could summarize
everything we needed to know about arcsin
by this statement,
we can do the same thing for arctan.
We can write that
x = arctan(y) IF AND ONLY IF y = tan(x),
but this time the
intervals are different.
This equivalence is for all x between
-π/2 and π/2 NOT including the endpoints,
and for ALL real numbers y.
We have one more function to go.
Let’s look at cosine.
Here is the graph.
Like the other trig functions,
cos is not one-to-one.
But we can restrict the domain
to make it one-to-one.
In this case we cannot pick
a domain that is symmetric around 0.
It is customary to restrict cos
to the function defined by this
piece of the graph, in red.
And we call the inverse
of this restriction ARCCOSINE.
By now you should be
having a feeling of deja-vu.
So perhaps you can do the rest?
Can you define arccosine?
Can you sketch its graph?
And can you write
the corresponding statement,
like the ones I wrote
for arcsin and arctan?
To conclude the video,
let’s look at derivatives.
In the previous video I already found
the derivative of arcsin.
If you imitate the
exact same derivation,
you will obtain that arccos has
the same derivative as arcsin,
but with an additional MINUS SIGN.
Notice that the + for arcsin,
and the - for arccos,
are due to the specific choices
of restrictions that we made.
If you choose a different restriction
of sin or cos,
you may get a different result.
And finally, the derivative of arctan(x),
with respect to x, is 1/(1 + x²).
I recommend memorizing at least the
derivatives of arcsin and arctan.
They appear often in many situations,
and they are helpful.
For example,
they are useful when computing integrals.
Perhaps you are wondering:
what about the other trig functions?
What about arcsecant,
arccosecant, and arccotangent?
We could define them in a similar way.
However, in practice, these last
three functions are rarely used.
For most practical purposes,
arcsin, arccos, and arctan
are everything we need.
So I won’t say anything else about them.
Chapter 5:
The Mean Value Theorem is perhaps the most
interesting result in calculus.
And yet, when you look at the statement, at
first sight it appears not very interesting,
technical, boring...
Honestly, it appears useless.
So why do we care? Or, should we care?
In this video I want to try to explain why
this theorem, despite its appearances,
is very important.
The goal of the Mean Value Theorem is
to help us obtain information about a function
using something about its derivative.
When we know something about the derivative,
what can we say about the function?
For example, here is a question related to
it.
We know that constant functions have zero
derivative.
Are those the only ones?
Are there other functions with zero derivative?
It appears not.
So as a theorem, are constant functions the
only ones with zero derivative?
Here it is written as a theorem.
If I take an open interval and a function
defined on it...
IF I assume the derivative is always zero
on it, THEN the function must be constant.
This is an example - 
the easiest example I can think of -
of a theorem where we use something 
about the derivative
to conclude something
about the function.
This is the simplest such example.
Since it is the simplest, to begin with
let's prove this little theorem.
.... Hmmm ...
Surprisingly, I do not even how to start!
It turns out that despite looking very simple,
this is not easy to prove.
Pause the video if you need to and try to
do it.
There is no clear way to prove this.
Actually, what is happening is that we
do not know ANY way
to prove this simple theorem 
without using the Mean Value Theorem.
And if we do not know any way to prove this,
then we do not know any way to prove many
more things that are much more complicated.
Any interesting theorem in calculus
about applications of the derivatives
are not as easy to prove as they look.
In fact, they ALL rely on the Mean Value Theorem.
In fact, EVERYTHING relies on the Mean Value
Theorem.
Here is a list of just some of the consequences
of the Mean Value Theorem.
To prove that if a function has zero derivative,
it must be constant: that's the MVT.
To prove that if a function has positive derivative,
then it must be increasing: that's the MVT.
All integration methods rely on the
MVT.
L'Hopital's Rule, if you are familiar with
it, relies on a variant of the MVT.
The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus...
...Taylor's theorem, for writing functions as
Taylor series with the right remainder...
...or the multivariable calculus result about 
cross-double derivatives being equal...
...and pretty much anything important
in calculus, whether it's big or small,
requires the MVT to prove it.
So, it is worth learning and proving the Mean
Value Theorem.
But that is quite an investment, because
to get to the Mean Value Theorem,
we have to take a few steps. I am not
going to state what this theorem says yet.
Before we are ready to state and prove the
Mean Value Theorem we need a little lemma:
a simplified version that is called
Rolle's Theorem.
It is interesting on its own, but the main
reason we'll study Rolle's Theorem
is as a stepping stone to get to the MVT.
And in order to prove Rolle's Theorem we need
two other theorems:
One that we already know, the Extreme Value Theorem, and another one that,
for lack of a better name, I am going to call the Local Extreme Value Theorem.
So this is going to be the path that we take
with the sole goal of eventually getting to
the Mean Value Theorem... and then there will
be profit.
All the applications we want will come out
of the Mean Value Theorem.
So, what about you?
Should you care about the Mean Value Theorem?
To be honest, it depends. Perhaps yes, perhaps
not.
What is your goal when you study calculus?
If you just want to use calculus tools, I
suggest you skip the MVT.
You do not need it, you will never use it.
It's not worth it.
But if you want to learn to prove the theorems,
and to know why they are true
and prove them rigorously, then 
you absolutely need the MVT,
since there is little or nothing that you
can prove without it.

In this video, I will explain the difference
between LOCAL extremum and EXTREMUM.
I will also state the Local Extreme Value Theorem.
This theorem is useful on its own, 
but it is also the first step
in our journey towards the Mean Value Theorem.
Before I define Local Extremum, let's look
at an example.
Consider the function f, defined by this graph.
Its domain is the interval [0, 5], including
both endpoints.
This function has a minimum and a maximum.
The minimum happens at this point P.
I can say that
"f has a minimum of 1 at x = 3."
The maximum happens at this point Q.
It is an endpoint, but there is nothing
wrong with that.
I can say that
"f has a maximum of 5 at x = 5."
But what about this third point R?
The function does not have a maximum at R.
The value at R is 3, at the function takes
values greater than 3 somewhere else.
However, if I stay close to R only...
...if I only look at the piece of the graph
near R...
...then it looks like a maximum.
I am going to call this point a 
LOCAL maximum.
In this example, I say that f has a local
maximum at x = 1.
Let's define this concept rigorously.
Let f be a function with domain I,
and let c be a point in I.
I want to define what it means for f to have
a maximum at c,
and to have a local maximum at c.
We already knew the definition of maximum.
We say that f has a maximum at c when f(x) is
less than or equal to f(c)
for all values of x in I.
Now, for the new concept!
We say that f has a local maximum at c when...
For every x close to c, f(x) is less than
or equal to f(c).
It is fine to leave the definition as is, but I
can also take this piece,
"for every x close to c," and rewrite it
more formally.
"For every x close to c" means "for every x
on some interval centred at c."
Or, equivalently,
It means that there exists some
positive radius δ (delta)
such that IF the distance between x and c is
less than δ,
THEN f(x) is less than or equal to f(c).
That is an entirely equivalent way of
writing the definition.
There are also analogous definitions for
minimum and local minimum.
I will not write them explicitly, but I will assume
you can write them.
This definition requires some comments,
mostly about terminology.
The word "extremum" means 
"maximum or minimum."
If I want to talk about both at once, I can
simply say "extremum."
Some people prefer to say "global extremum,"
instead of simply "extremum,"
to emphasize the difference between
local and global.
"Global extremum" means the same thing as
simply "extremum."
The plurals of extremum, maximum and minimum are
extrema, maxima and minima.
They are irregular plurals.
And the most important note:
according to the definition I am using,
endpoints NEVER count as local extrema,
because the function needs to be defined to
the left AND to the right of c.
This...is controversial.
Some books prefer to use a different definition, 
which I am not writing.
And that other definition includes endpoints.
There are good reasons to go either way.
The majority of calculus textbooks use the
definition in this video,
(and that is why I will be using it,) which does
not include endpoints.
But the majority of analysis textbooks use
the other one. It is a matter of convention.
So remember that when you are 
reading math somewhere else,
they may be using a different convention.
There is one specific point where this
distinction is VERY important,
and it makes a big difference.
I will point to it later in this video.
Now that we have our definitions, let's move 
towards the local extreme value theorem.
I want to explore this question:
What can we say about the derivative of
a function at a local extremum?
The function in this graph has a
local maximum.
The tangent line at the local maximum is
horizontal, and the derivative is zero.
It also has a local minimum,
and the derivative at the local minimum
is ALSO zero.
It looks like this will always happen at
local extrema.
Well...not always.
Here is the graph of a function that has a
local maximum and a local minimum,
but which does not have derivative zero
at those points,
because it has CORNERS at those points, and
the derivative does not exist.
These seem to be the only two options,
and that is what the Local Extreme
Value Theorem says.
Specifically: Let f be a function with domain
an interval I,
and let c be a point in I.
IF f has a local extremum at c, and c is an
interior point (not an endpoint),
THEN the derivative f'(c) either is zero
or does not exist.
One very important observation:
Given the definition of local extremum that I
am using, it was not necessary to say
explicitly that c must be an interior point.
I could omit the second hypothesis,
because it was already included in the
first hypothesis
that f had a local extremum at c.
However, I chose to be redundant and write
it explicitly anyway to emphasize it.
If you use the other definition (the one
that allows for endpoints),
then you definitely must include the
second hypothesis.
In summary: whether you allow endpoints to
be called local extrema or not,
(I do not.)
you MUST exclude endpoints from
this theorem.
Having clarified that, one last definition.
We often give a name to these special
points:
We say that c is a CRITICAL POINT of f
when it is an interior point of the domain,
and the derivative f'(c) is either zero or does not exist.
With this notation, the theorem 
simply says
that local extrema are always
critical points.
Notice that the converse is not true.
A critical point is not always a local extremum.
Now that we understand the theorem,
we need to prove it.
That will be the next video.
While the Local Extreme Value Theorem
was mostly our starting point
towards the Mean Value Theorem, it is also
important on its own,
and it has its own applications.
In a later video, I will show an application:
how to find the local and global extrema
of a function.

In this video, I will write a proof of the
Local Extreme Value Theorem.
In the previous video (linked in the description),
I already introduced the theorem.
I explained the statement and I gave
some motivation.
In this video I will ONLY write the proof.
As a reminder, this is the theorem.
It says that IF a function f has a local
extremum at a point c,
which is an interior point to the domain,
not an endpoint,
THEN the derivative f'(c) is zero or
does not exist.
This is exactly the statement I wrote in
the previous video.
Time for a proof.
There are actually two proofs!
One if f has a local maximum, and one if
it has a local minimum.
I will write a proof only for a local 
maximum. The other case is very similar.
We know that the derivative f'(c)
is defined as 
lim_(x → c) [f(x) – f(c)]/(x – c)
I want to prove that EITHER this limit
does not exist or equals zero.
To do so, I am going to assume
the limit exists,
and then I will prove that it must be zero.
That will be enough.
I will examine what happens to this limit 
as x approaches c
on the right and on the left separately.
First, the denominator.
When x approaches c on the right,
(x – c) is positive.
When x approaches c on the left,
(x – c) is negative.
Then, the numerator.
The numerator has the same sign
in both cases.
[f(x) – f(c)] must be negative or zero
as x approaches c on both sides.
This is my definition of local maximum.
f(c) must be greater than or equal to f(x)
when x is close to c.
As a consequence, on the right of c, the
QUOTIENT is negative or zero,
and on the left of c, the quotient is
positive or zero.
What can we say about the side limits?
The limit on the right must be negative or zero.
A priori, it could also not exist, but we
are assuming the full limit exists,
so the two side limits must also exist,
and be equal.
...and the limit on the left must be
positive or zero.
For the two side limits to exist and be
equal, they must both equal zero.
That is it!
That completes the proof.
I want to add some comments.
First, notice that we needed c to be an
interior point,
because we needed to examine the limit
on both sides of c.
If c had been an endpoint of the domain,
the proof would not have worked.
Second, this was a proof only for a
local maximum.
I invite you to think about how to prove
the result for a local minimum.
It is very similar.
You only need to change the direction of
some of the inequalities.
Everything else works the same way.
Finally, I remind you that I have proven

...and g(1) is positive.

from positive, to negative, to positive

must be at least one zero between -2 and 0,
and there must be at least one zero
between 0 and 1.

of the interval are the same.

the derivative f'
must have a zero between x_1 and x_2.
We have drawn an important conclusion.

in the derivative.

x_1 smaller than x_2, smaller than x_3.

then the derivative must have at least
three zeroes...
one between each pair of consecutive
zeroes of f.
We have drawn a second
important conclusion.
The derivative f' must have at least as
many zeroes
as the function f does, minus one.
Or, equivalently, the number of zeroes
of the function is AT MOST
the number of zeroes of its derivative,
plus one.
This is the same inequality
as conclusion 2.
This is important enough that I am going
to call it a theorem.
The only hypothesis we had assumed
was that
f must be continuous and differentiable
on an interval.
This is exactly what I needed!
I now have a way to say that the number

x^4 is always non-negative.

is always strictly positive.

number of zeroes of a function
is at most the number of zeroes of its
derivative, plus one.
Let's use it on g' as the function!

is at most the number of zeroes of its

with g as the function.
The number of zeroes of g
is at most the number of zeroes of its

which is less than or equal to

Here the base is x, but the height is y, 
so this wall has area xy,
and the same is true of the wall on the 
back, and the one on the left,
and the one on the front.
So this adds 4xy 
to the total surface area.
That's the quantity I want to minimize.
However, x and y are not independent
variables; they are related.
There is an extra constraint because we
want the total volume of the box
to be a fixed quantity.
We want the total volume to be 500cm³.
Now the volume of a box is the product of
the three dimensions,
so it's going to be x·x·y or x²y.
I think I should also say that x must be
a positive number
and y must be a positive number,
otherwise the problem 
doesn't make any sense physically.
And that's it. Now I have a function with
two variables but with a constraint.
I find it easier to calculate 
the maximum or the minimum
of a function with a single variable, without constraints,
and one way to attack this 
is I can use this constraint
to write one of the variables 
in terms of the other.
In this case I think it's a bit easier to
write y as 500/x²,
and then I can use this and plug it in the
original expression for the surface area
to get s in terms of x and x only.
If I do that, I get that s is going to be
x² + 4x·500/x²,

x² + 2000/x.

find the minimum of this function,
and this is in the domain when x is any
positive number.
That's the only constraint I have,
now that I have removed y.
Now that we have set up the problem,

domain, so to find the minimum
I look at when the derivative is
zero or positive or negative.

going to be 2x - 2000/x².
Now, I'm interested in when this is zero
or positive or negative,
and I find it easier if I
reduce to common denominator.
I can also factor a 2,
and in the top I get x³ - 1000.
And once I put it this way it's clear:
the derivative is zero exactly when x³ is

That's the only critical point.
At this point, I know many students like

This is the only critical point, but we're

and when x is smaller than 10,

Therefore, the function s is decreasing
all the way to 10
and increasing all the way after 10.
And now yes, with this information I know
if the function is all the way decreasing

and therefore at 10, definitely, I need to

Not a local minimum only, just the global
minimum, which is what I was looking for.
So after doing this little justification
then I know I have found the answer.
And therefore my answer is x = 10cm, and
remember I also need the other dimension,

An optimization problem is an application
of calculus to a physical situation
where I want to make a certain quantity as
large as possible or as small as possible.
Here is an example.
A woman gets on her boat at point A on
one side of a straight river, 3km wide.
She wants to get to point B,
8km downstream on the opposite side.
She could row the boat directly across the
river and then run for 8km,
or she could row directly from A to B,
or she could row
to some intermediate point and then run.
If she can row 6km/h and run 8km/h,
where should she land 
to reach B as soon as possible?
At this moment I invite you to pause the
video and try to solve the problem.
Even if you cannot fully solve it, if you
spend some time trying to get started,
setting it up, drawing a picture, and
understanding what it means,
my explanation will be much more useful.
The first part of the problem
is the modelling.
We need to transform the word problem
into a mathematical precise question.
In this case the question will be
something like:
I want to find the maximum or the minimum
of this specific function
on this specific domain.
Let's draw a picture.
We have a straight river,
and the woman begins on one side of the
river, let's call this point A.
We also know
the width of the river is 3km,
and she wants to row to some point
on the other side, let's call it B,
and we know B is 8km downstream from A.
Now as the problem says, she could row
straight and then run,
or row directly to B, or most likely,
to some intermediate point.
So the path she is going to take is
probably rowing somewhere there
and then running all the way to B.
The other information we have is that
she rows at 6km/h and she runs at 8km/h.
And what we want to figure out is
the position of this point here.
So let me use a variable for it.
I could do it in various ways;
I could for example use this angle
as the variable.
I think it's going to be simpler
to call x this distance,
and that's what I want to figure out,
what the value of x is.
Now we need an equation
for the total time,
because that's what
I'm trying to minimize,
the total time she spends 
moving from A to B.
And I can say that the total time
is going to be broken into
the two parts of the trip.
Let me give a name to this point:
let's say we call it C.
The total time is the time from A to C
plus the time from C to B.
And now what I need is to write
an explicit equation for each one of them.
For that I will need to know the value
of the two distances she runs and rows.
The distance from C to B is 8 - x.
As for the distance from A to C, 
I have a right triangle here:
this side is 3, this side is x,
so using Pythagoras' theorem,
I know that this length is √(x²+9), and
I think now I have all the data I need.
I can write the total time as follows:
first I need the time from A to C.
Well the distance is √(x²+9),
and the velocity for rowing is 6km/h,
therefore the time will be the distance,
√(x²+9), over the velocity, 6.
And then to go from C to B,
the time from C to B is going to be 
the distance, 8 - x,
divided by the velocity for running, which is 8 km/h,
and that's the total time.
I should specify the domain.
I cannot forget that.
So the values of x
have to be between 0 and 8.
It wouldn't make sense to row to a point
here to the left or to a point past B,
so it's going to be between 0 and 8.
And I definitely need to
include the endpoints.
I'm looking for
the minimum of this function,
and it could very well happen at 0,
or it could very well happen at 8,
maybe those are the optimal ways to go.
And now I have it: this is the function
I want to minimize in this domain.
That completes the setup of the problem.
The second part of the problem is easier:
we just need to use calculus
to find the minimum of this function.
Before we jump happily
into taking derivatives,
I'd like to make an observation.
This function is continuous,
and the domain is a closed and bounded
interval, it includes both endpoints.
Therefore I know, 
by using the extreme value theorem,
that this function must have a minimum.
And I know more:
the minimum will either be a critical
point or it will be one of the endpoints.
Therefore all I have to do is find the
critical points, find the endpoints,
and compare them, and I'll be done.
I don't need to do any more justification.
But that is only because I could use
the extreme value theorem,
because the function was continuous and
the domain included both endpoints.
Well I know what the endpoints are,
so let's find the critical points.
For that I need to figure out
what the derivative is.
And now I need to figure out
when the derivative is 0.
So I will escape the details,
do the algebra at your own pace,
but after doing a bit of calculation,
this function ends up having
one single critical point:
the derivative is 0 exactly when
x is 9/√7 km.
Now this is the only critical point
inside the domain.
There could be others outside the domain,
but I don't care;
I only care about what happens
inside the domain.
So I have my critical point,
and I also have my two endpoints,
so let's compare them.
And as a comment, yes,
we definitely need to use the endpoints.
It's possible the minimum is there.
For example,
if the woman could row

and plug them back into
the equation for T.
That requires a little bit of calculation.

When x = 0, the time is 1.5h.
When x equals 9/√7,

the time is 1 + (√7)/8 h,

The minimum of this function

In this video, I will introduce
indeterminate forms for limits.
I want to explain what it means, and
what it DOESN'T mean,
to have an indeterminate form.
In the later videos,
linked in the description,
I will explain how to resolve them;
how to actually compute these limits.
For now, I only want to explain
what an indeterminate form is.
As motivation, 
consider this limit.
The function is a quotient.
The numerator and denominator are
both nice, continuous functions.
So, I may be tempted to evaluate it.
But if I simply evaluate, I get...
zero divided by zero?!
That is not the answer!
That is not a number!
That makes no sense.
Instead, we have an
indeterminate form.
This indeterminate form appears
when I try to compute
certain types of quotients.
Let's say I want to compute
the limit of f divided by g,
and I know something about f and g.
IF I know the limit of f is two,
and the limit of g is three,
What can I conclude about
the limit of the quotient?
That is easy. The limit of the 
quotient is 2/3.
That is a simple use
of limit laws.
Another example.
IF I know the limit of f is 1,
and the limit of g is infinity,
what can I conclude about the
limit of the quotient?
In this case, the limit of
the quotient is zero.
We can prove this from the
definition of the limit, if necessary.
However...
IF I know the limit of f is zero,
and the limit of g is also zero,
what can I conclude about the
limit of the quotient?
In this case, I cannot
draw any conclusions
about the limit of the quotient.
Just based on the information I have,
I do not know what the limit will be.
The way to prove this
is with examples.
I can construct various examples
that are all of this type,
zero divided by zero,
but where the final answer,
the limit of the quotient,
is different in each case.
Let's do that.
All of these limits will be taken
as x approaches zero.
The limit of 2x/x...is 2.
Just simplify the function
before computing the limit.
The limit of x^2 / x...
is 0.
Same argument here – simplify the
function before computing the limit.
And the limit of x / x^3...
is infinity.
That's it. That proves my claim.
In these three examples, the limit of
the numerator is always zero,
and the limit of the denominator
is always zero,
but the limit of the quotient
is different in each case.
We say that 0/0 is a limit
indeterminate form.
This means what I have at the top.
Nothing more, nothing less.
Indeterminate form means that
whenever we encounter this
type of limit – 0/0 – 
just from knowing that it is 0/0,
I cannot draw any conclusions.
I have some important
remarks to make.
First, we are talking about limits.
We are never ACTUALLY
dividing zero by zero.
For any value of x, I will have
something CLOSE to zero,
divided by something
CLOSE to zero.
Second, indeterminate form does
not mean that the limit of the quotient
is undefined or that it does not exist.
Each particular case may be different.
Some may exist and some may not.
It only means that we do not know
what the answer is yet,
and we have to do more work.
And third, indeterminate
forms normally arise
because there is a tension between
two pieces of the function.
In this case, the numerator thinks:
"zero divided by anything is zero!"
So the numerator wants to make
the quotient have limit zero.
The denominator thinks:
"anything divided by zero is ±infinity!"
So, the denominator wants to
make the quotient
have a limit of ±infinity.
This becomes a competition
between f and g,
to see which one approaches zero
faster...so, which one "pulls" faster.
All other indeterminate forms
can also be understood
as this kind of tension between
two pieces of the function.
The most common
indeterminate forms are:
zero over zero,
plus or minus infinity
over plus or minus infinity,
zero times infinity,
infinity minus infinity,
and the exponential types:
zero to the power of zero,
infinity to the zero,
and one to the power of ±infinity.
A very useful tool to solve some
indeterminate forms is L'Hôpital's Rule.
In the next sequence of videos,
I will explain what L'Hôpital's Rule says,
present examples of how to use it,
explain how L'Hôpital's Rule
may fail if misused,
and go over the different types of
indeterminate forms
to learn tricks to handle each one.
Before I do that, I want to comment
on one more type of limit:
one divided by zero.
Why didn't I include it in the list?
If the limit of the numerator is one,
and the limit of the denominator is zero,
what can we conclude about
the limit of the quotient?
We do not normally consider this
an indeterminate form,
because we CAN conclude something.
But, we have to be careful.
In this situation, I may be tempted to
say that the limit of the quotient
is going to be infinity.
But I can ONLY conclude that the limit
of the absolute value of the quotient
is going to be infinity,
because the denominator may be
positive or negative!
If I know that, in addition, near the point
the denominator stays positive,
then I conclude the limit
of the quotient is infinity.
But if I know that, in addition, near the
point, the denominator stays negative,
then I conclude the limit
of the quotient is negative infinity.
For example, let's say I want to compute
the limit as x approach 3 on the right
of 1/(x – 3)(2 – x).
This is one divided by zero.
Let's tally the sign of the denominator.
As x approaches 3 from the right
(x is greater than 3),
x – 3 is...positive.
And 2 – x is negative.
So the full denominator is negative.
So, the limit is minus infinity.
So we have to be careful,
but we can say something,
and that is why I did not include 1/0
on the list of indeterminate forms.

In this video, I will introduce the
theorem known as L'Hôpital's Rule.
I want to explain what the theorem says,
and when it does and doesn't apply.
Then, I will work out some examples
in the next video.
L'Hôpital's Rule is a tool that
helps us compute limits.
Specifically, let's look at these four
examples on the screen.
They are all limits of quotients,
and they are all indeterminate forms,
of types zero/zero
or infinity/infinity.
They are all examples where, to compute
the limit, you cannot just look
at the numerator and
denominator separately,
because we always get
an indeterminate form.
L'Hôpital's Rule provides use with
something we can do to attack all these,
so that we are not stuck.
So, what does this theorem say?
I'm going to begin with two functions,
f and g, and a real number a.
I want to compute that the limit
as x approaches a
of f(x) divided by g(x).
Well, the theorem actually is more
general than that.
The theorem also works for limits
at infinity, and limits at minus infinity,
and also for side limits.
All those types.
For simplicity, I'm going to write
the statement only for limits
as x approaches a number a.
But, the theorem is still true
for all those other types of limits.
This theorem says that under certain
conditions, which I will go into in a moment,
the limit of f divided by g
is the same as the limit of f' divided by g'.
How would this be helpful?
Normally, the situation is that I want to
compute the first limit,
and that's a difficult limit
which we don't know how to compute.
So, instead we compute the second limit,
which hopefully is easier.
And that happens often, because when
we take derivatives of functions,
they simplify a little bit.
Now, what are the conditions for
this theorem to work?
The most important one,
(I wouldn't put it first, but it's
the most important one)
is that this only works when the limit
I am trying to compute,
the limit of of f/g,
is an indeterminate form of type
zero/zero or ±infinity/±infinity.
What that means is that
for this to work,
either the limit of the numerator and
the limit of the denominator
are both zero, separately,
OR each one of them in separately
either plus or minus infinity.
Notice that those are the only
indeterminate forms of quotients.
So, if I am in any other situation, then
I am not allowed to use L'Hôpital's Rule.
But luckily, if I am in any other situation,
I don't need L'Hôpital's Rule,
because I wouldn't have an indeterminate
form, and then I would be done.
I would have no doubt about
what the limit was.
So, most importantly, this rule only applies
when I get an indeterminate form:
zero/zero or ±infinity/±infinity.
Apart from this, there are two
technical conditions
for the rule to even make sense.
First, I need to require f and g to be
differentiable as x approaches a.
Otherwise, it makes no sense
to take their derivatives.
When I say "as x approaches a,"
I mean when x is close to a, 
but not necessarily at a.
It doesn't matter if they
are not differentiable at a.
I am also going to require that g and g'
are never zero as x approaches a.
So again, when x is close to a,
but not necessarily a.
Otherwise, I cannot even write
the function f/g, or f'/g',
when x is close to a, because
it would be dividing by zero.
If I cannot write those functions, then
I cannot make sense of the limits.
So those are technical conditions,
without which the question is not
even worth positing.
But, there is a fourth condition,
which we often forget
because it doesn't come into play
often, but which is very important.
For this theorem to be true,
I also need to request
that the second limit,
the limit of f'/g'
either exists, is infinity,
or is minus infinity.
Otherwise, the theorem
does not apply.
Let me elaborate on this. As I said,
this is something we often forget.
This means that if the second limit,
the limit of f'/g', exists,
then I can conclude that the limit of
f/g also exists, and is equal.
If the limit of f'/g' is infinity,
then I can conclude that the
limit of f/g is infinity as well.
If the limit of f'/g' is minus infinity,
then I can conclude that the
limit of f/g is minus infinity.
But!
If the limit of f'/g' does not exist
for a different reason,
say, it is some function that
oscillates in a weird way,
and that's why the limit doesn't exist,
then I can draw no conclusion
about the original limit!
The original limit may well exist, and
be anything...or perhaps it doesn't exist.
So, for the theorem to apply, I need
the second limit to either exist,
or be plus or minus infinity.
That's it! That's the theorem.
What about the proof?
Well, the proof is rather long.
This is not just one theorem...
it is a lot of theorems.
It's a separate theorem for 0/0
and for infinity/infinity,
and there is also a separate theorem
for limits at a point a (a real number),
and at infinity, or minus infinity,
or side limits...there are a lot of them.
We normally prove
one of them from scratch
and then reduce the rest
of the versions to it.
The proof of this theorem relies
on the Mean Value Theorem,
or rather, on a variant of the
Mean Value Theorem.
I'm not going to present the proof here.
If you are interested, it appears
in every calculus textbook.
Instead, I would rather focus on
presenting examples
and showing how to use this
to actually compute limits.
So, in the next video, I will work out
a few examples from beginning to end,
to see how to compute limits
with this theorem.
In the one after that, I will
present an example on when
the pesky Condition 4
comes into play:
a situation where we are not
allowed to use L'Hôpital's Rule,
but if we do not notice, and use
it anyway, we get a wrong result.

Chapter 9:
The Substitution Rule is a method
to compute antiderivatives.
It is just an easy-to-use algorithm to
make things a bit simpler,
and a bit more systematic
than guess-and-check.
In the next video, I will present a few
examples of how to use it.
However,
in this video,
I want to explain where the Substitution
Rule comes from and why it works.
As motivation, I will first investigate
some examples by guess-and-check,
then I will derive the general rule,
and finally I will explain
the notation that we commonly use.
To begin, let’s have a look at
these two antiderivatives.
First, I have the antiderivative
of x⁴, with respect to x.
I know this is (1/5)⋅x⁵,
+ an integration constant.
Why? We can check it.
The derivative of (1/5)⋅x⁵ is (1/5)⋅(5x⁴)
which is x⁴, as I wanted.
That’s easy.
Next, what is the
antiderivative of (ln(x))⁴?
Perhaps, it is something
like (1/5)⋅(ln(x))⁵?
We can check it.
The derivative of (1/5)⋅(ln(x))⁵
is (1/5)⋅5(ln(x))⁴...
times (1/x)!
Don’t forget the chain rule!
That’s unfortunate.
So this function is not an
antiderivative of this function.
However, if I had an extra 1/x in the
integrand, then it would be correct!
The 1/x was essential!
(Again, because of the chain rule.)
I can generalize this a bit more:
what is an antiderivative of
(g(x))⁴ for ANY function g?
Well, I don't know,
but IF instead I have (g(x))⁴⋅g'(x),
THEN an antiderivative is
(1/5)⋅(g(x))⁵.
Why? Let’s check it!
The derivative of (1/5)⋅(g(x))⁵,
is (1/5)⋅5(g(x))⁴⋅g'(x).
(Again, chain rule.)
And this will work for any function g,
as long as g is differentiable.
Let’s generalize it even more
in the form of a nice theorem.
IF I know that an
antiderivative of f is F,
THEN, what is an
antiderivative of f(g(x))?
I don’t know, but an antiderivative
of f(g(x))⋅g'(x) is F(g(x)).
Why is that?
The IF part means that
the derivative of F is f.
The THEN part means that the derivative
of F(g(x)) is f(g(x))⋅g'(x).
Notice that the second statement...
...follows directly from the chain rule
PLUS the first statement.
This is sometimes called
the Substitution Rule,
but I think it should be called
the backwards chain rule.
All I did was take the chain rule,
(which is a rule about derivatives),
and reversed it to get
a rule about antiderivatives.
I used the chain rule backwards!
To make this into a proper theorem,
I should introduce my variables:
f, g, and F are functions, and I need to
assume that F and g are differentiable.
And that’s it.
That's the Substitution Rule.
This is the theory.
In practice, we try to use this theorem
in an efficient and algorithmic way...
...that simplifies computations,
rather than just guess-and-check.
I will explain this next.
Assume we know that an
antiderivative of f is F,
and we want to compute
∫f(g(x))⋅g'(x)dx.
I want to think of g(x)
(the argument of f), as the new variable.
For that purpose, I will give it a name:
I will call it "u";
because I want to think that I am
actually integrating f(u),
with respect to u,
so I need to write "du" here.
If I do this, it seems that I am
re-writing g'(x)⋅dx as du.
This is what we call a substitution:
u = g(x),
and du = g'(x)⋅dx.
For now, this is just notation.
There is a way to give a precise
meaning to the differential
and to make this rigorous,
using so-called differential forms,
but that is beyond the scope of
this course, so I won’t go there.
I will just think of it as notation.
Ok then, if I do this substitution,
the integral becomes ∫f(u)du.
Now I find an antiderivative,
which by assumption
is F(u) + an integration constant.
And after integrating,
I UNDO the substitution,
and I replace u back with g(x).
The justification for this notation is
that it gives us the right result.
(This is the same result
I derived earlier.)
And the point of the notation is
that it streamlines the process,
so we don't have to use
so much guess-and-check.
This will become clearer with an example.
Let's go back to the original example.
I want to compute ∫(ln(x))⁴⋅(1/x)dx,
but this time I will
do it using substitution.

written in terms of ln(x),

(1/x) is also here.

substitution: u = ln(x).
If I do that, I also need to
substitute du as (1/x)dx.
Great! This works because
I already have 1/x here!
Then, this becomes ∫u⁴du.
I know how to do this.
The answer is (1/5)⋅u⁵,
plus an integration constant.
And now that I have completed the
integration, I undo the substitution:
and I am back to (1/5)⋅(ln(x))⁵,
plus the integration constant.
In general,
every time the integrand is written in
terms of a function (like ln(x) here),
and the derivative is already
in the integrand (1/x in this case),
using a substitution like this
is a good idea.
And after doing the substitution,
we hope to find an easier integral
that we can complete.
As to why the method works, that is the
whole explanation I gave earlier.
It all boils down to the chain rule.
To make this clear, I should work
through a few more examples next.

in the next video.

Integration by parts is a method to

to make things a bit simpler...

to be x⋅eˣ itself.

but I am hoping it is
RELATED to the right answer.

(let's use product rule)
1⋅eˣ
(when I differentiate the first term)
+ x⋅eˣ
(when I differentiate the second term).
And now look at what happened:
the derivative I want
is the second term in this sum.
This gives me an idea!
If I can find a second function
whose derivative is exactly eˣ,

subtract the two functions,

with a certain antiderivative.

I could find a function whose
derivative was what I wanted,
plus an extra term.
And then I had to
subtract a second function,
whose derivative was the extra term.
This process is basically the idea
behind integration by parts.
Integration by parts
gives us a formula...
...to do the same thing
in a more systematic way,
(without so much guess-and-check)
but it is the same idea.
To learn what this new formula is, let’s
have a closer look at the product rule.
The product rule says,
that the derivative of the product of two
differentiable functions f(x)⋅g(x),
is the sum of two terms:
f'(x)⋅g(x) + f(x)⋅g'(x).
This is the product rule
that we know very well.
It is a rule about derivatives.
Every rule about derivatives...
...can be converted into
a rule about ANTIDERIVATIVES.
I can instead say that an antiderivative
of f'(x)⋅g(x) + f(x)⋅g'(x),
is f(x)⋅g(x).
That means exactly the same thing.
Unfortunately, this equation
as a method to compute antiderivatives,
directly as it is written,
won’t be very useful.
It won’t come in handy that often.

trying to compute an integral,
that the function is
the sum of two terms,

That won’t happen often.
However, I can re-write it
in a more convenient way.
I can use linearity of integrals
to write the left-hand side...
...as the sum of two integrals.
And if I isolate one of them, I get that:
∫f(x)⋅g'(x)dx,
is f(x)⋅g(x) - ∫f'(x)⋅g(x)dx.
Notice that I am no longer
writing the integration constant.
In this last identity,
both the left-hand side and the right

which already carries
an arbitrary additive constant.
So the constant is already
included on both sides,
and I do not need to write it explicitly.
This identity is going to be more useful.
It allows me to transform
one integral into another integral.
(And hopefully the second integral will

I am going to call this a theorem.

formal definition of this concept.

for which values of n?

The “Big Theorem” is probably the most
important theorem about sequences
that you will learn
in a calculus course.
It helps us compute
many limits very quickly.
In this video, I will explain the
statement of the theorem,
and how to use it.
In the next video,
I will write a proof.
As motivation, let’s look at a type of
limits that we understand very well:
rational sequences.
For example, let’s say I need to compute
lim_(n → ∞) (n³+2n+1)/(5n³+3n²).
You probably know how to do this.
When I have a quotient of polynomials,
the only thing that
matters is the biggest power.
In the numerator,
the biggest power is n³,
in the denominator,
the biggest power is also n³,
so this is the same as the
lim_(n → ∞) n³/5n³, which is 1/5.
That is the quick calculation, but let’s
remind ourselves of WHY this works.
To justify this method, the trick is
always to factor the biggest power.
Let’s factor n³ in both the
numerator and denominator.
I can cancel these terms,
and let’s see what we have left...
As (n → ∞), 1/n² has limit 0,
And so does 1/n³ and 1/n.
Now use the limit laws,
And that is how we get 1/5.
The key observation here is that the
lim_(n → ∞) n/n³,
is the limit of 1/n²,
which is 0.
(And the same is true for the
limits of the other quotients.)
And that is why, when I have a sum
of an n³ term and an n term,
the n term is irrelevant, and
the n³ term determines the answer.
(The same happens with the
other “irrelevant” terms.)
This is useful.
Can I generalize it?
I would like to do the same thing with
other sequences, not just powers.
I want to compare other terms and decide
which ones are more important,
and which ones can, so-to-speak,
be ignored when added together?
That’s what the Big Theorem is about.
First I need a new definition.
We will use this new concept
only for POSITIVE sequences.
I am going to write that
a sequence aₙ is << a sequence bₙ,
To mean that the lim_(n → ∞) aₙ/bₙ = 0.
This is just new notation.
And when this happens, I am going to say
that “aₙ is much smaller than bₙ”,
or that “bₙ is much larger than aₙ”.
For example, with this
new notation and definition,
I can say that n is much smaller than n³,
because the limit of the quotient is 0.
Now I have the tools
to state the Big Theorem.
The Big Theorem establishes
a hierarchy among sequences.
It says that a logarithm is much smaller
than any power with a positive exponent,
which is much smaller than any
exponential with base > 1,
which is much smaller than n!,
which is much smaller than nⁿ (which
I will call a “power-exponential”).
That’s the Big Theorem.
Why is this useful? We can use it
to compute many limits quickly.
Let’s look at an example.
I want to compute the
lim_(n → ∞) (eⁿ +2⋅n¹⁰⁰)/(ln(n) + 5⋅eⁿ).
According to the Big Theorem,
n¹⁰⁰ is much smaller than eⁿ,
and ln(n) is much smaller than eⁿ,
so the only terms that
matter are eⁿ and 5⋅eⁿ.
And this limit is 1/5.
That’s it!
Why is this?
We can justify this rigorously
from the Big Theorem,
like I did in the example
at the beginning of the video.
To justify this, I will factor the
biggest term (in this case eⁿ),
in both the numerator and denominator.
And let’s look at what I have left.
n¹⁰⁰/eⁿ has limit 0 as n → ∞.
That is exactly what
the Big Theorem says!
The same applies to ln(n)/eⁿ.
Now use the limit laws, and we get that
the full limit is 1/5. That’s it!
I hope you can see from this example
that there are many other limits
that we can compute very quickly,
using this theorem.
Of course, we still need to prove it.
The proof will be in the next video.
